# Act 1: The Problem {.section}

---

## 01. The Golden Circle

<p class="slide-kicker">Direction of thinking and communicating</p>

<div class="bpm-compare">
<div class="bpm-flow muted">
<div class="bpm-lane">Default communication</div>
<div class="bpm-steps">
<div class="bpm-step">What we build</div>
<div class="bpm-arrow">-></div>
<div class="bpm-step">How it works</div>
<div class="bpm-arrow">-></div>
<div class="bpm-step weak">Why it matters</div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="bpm-flow accent">
<div class="bpm-lane">Golden Circle communication</div>
<div class="bpm-steps">
<div class="bpm-step primary">Why it matters</div>
<div class="bpm-arrow">-></div>
<div class="bpm-step">How we make it true</div>
<div class="bpm-arrow">-></div>
<div class="bpm-step">What we build</div>
</div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="references">
Reference: Simon Sinek, <a href="https://www.ted.com/talks/simon_sinek_how_great_leaders_inspire_action">How great leaders inspire action</a>, TEDxPuget Sound.
</div>

<aside class="notes">
This deck follows the Golden Circle deliberately. First establish the failure
mode Protopipe exists to address, then the operating model, then the platform
components.
</aside>

---

## 02. Protopipe's Golden Circle

<p class="slide-kicker">Fall in love with the problem, not the solution <span class="kicker-source"><a href="https://urilevine.com/book/">Uri Levine</a></span></p>

<div class="golden-visual">
<div class="golden-rings" aria-label="Protopipe Golden Circle">
<div class="golden-ring what">
<span class="ring-label">What</span>
<span class="ring-copy">Augmented Product Development Platform</span>
</div>
<div class="golden-ring how">
<span class="ring-label">How</span>
<span class="ring-copy">Experiments built in</span>
</div>
<div class="golden-ring why">
<span class="ring-label">Why</span>
<span class="ring-copy">Wrong and slow decisions</span>
</div>
</div>
<div class="golden-explainer">
<div class="golden-note why">
<strong>Why</strong>
Decisions without measurable feedback, invisible dependencies, and discussions without responsibility waste organizational time.
</div>
<div class="golden-note how">
<strong>How</strong>
Protopipe makes problems explicit, connects hypotheses to measurements, and turns responsibility into something observable.
</div>
<div class="golden-note what">
<strong>What</strong>
The platform makes this operating model tangible across strategy, product, architecture, development, monitoring, and learning.
</div>
</div>
</div>

---

## 03. The Strategy Is Defined. It Just Does Not Arrive.

<p class="slide-kicker">The problem is not missing intent. The problem is broken transmission.</p>

<div class="wound-grid">
<div class="wound-card">
<strong>Golden Circle exists</strong>
Many organizations have a purpose statement, values, principles, and strategic language.
</div>

<div class="wound-card">
<strong>Strategy exists</strong>
Steering meetings produce decisions, priorities, roadmaps, and transformation narratives.
</div>

<div class="wound-card critical">
<strong>Daily work does not change</strong>
Documentation becomes write-only. Culture shifts rarely reach teams formally, and even less often become internalized behavior.
</div>
</div>

<aside class="notes">
Press the wound: most organizations do not lack strategy artifacts. They lack a
living transmission layer that turns purpose, strategy, and culture into daily
decision behavior.
</aside>

---

## 04. Organizations Speak Different Codes

<p class="slide-kicker">The same signal becomes different communication in different systems.</p>

<div class="luhmann-codes">
<div class="code-card">
<span class="code-owner">Engineering</span>
<strong>Truth</strong>
<p>Does it work? Is it correct? Can we prove the failure?</p>
</div>

<div class="code-card">
<span class="code-owner">Finance</span>
<strong>Money</strong>
<p>What does it cost? Which budget moves? What is the business effect?</p>
</div>

<div class="code-card critical">
<span class="code-owner">C-Level / Management</span>
<strong>Power</strong>
<p>Can it be decided? Does it fit priorities, timing, ownership, and narrative?</p>
</div>

<div class="code-card">
<span class="code-owner">Product / Market</span>
<strong>Demand</strong>
<p>Does the market care? Which user behavior confirms or rejects it?</p>
</div>
</div>

<div class="translation-wound">
<div class="translation-row">
<span class="translation-from">Developer says</span>
<strong>“The system is built wrong.”</strong>
</div>
<div class="translation-row">
<span class="translation-from">C-Level hears</span>
<strong>“This threatens roadmap, budget, ownership, and commitment.”</strong>
</div>
<div class="translation-result">
Not the best idea wins. The most connectable communication wins.
</div>
</div>

<div class="references">
Theory anchor: Niklas Luhmann, organizational systems as communication systems. Applied reading: Alwin Mark, <a href="https://onlu.ch/warum-sich-in-unternehmen-nicht-die-besten-ideen-durchsetzen/">Warum sich in Unternehmen nicht die besten Ideen durchsetzen</a>, ONLU AG.
</div>

<aside class="notes">
Condense the Onlu/Luhmann argument: organizations are communication systems.
Departments and hierarchy levels use different codes. Engineering often speaks
truth; finance speaks money; management speaks power, prioritization, and
decision premises. Good ideas fail when they cannot connect across those codes.
</aside>

---

## 05. Deliberation Needs Observable Reality

<p class="slide-kicker">Decision meetings loop when there is no shared object to reason about.</p>

<div class="habermas-slide">
<div class="meeting-loop">
<div class="loop-step">More meetings</div>
<div class="loop-arrow">-></div>
<div class="loop-step">More slides</div>
<div class="loop-arrow">-></div>
<div class="loop-step">More opinions</div>
<div class="loop-arrow">-></div>
<div class="loop-step critical">Power word</div>
<div class="loop-arrow">-></div>
<div class="loop-step low-reward">Low-risk,<br>low-reward decision</div>
</div>


<div class="deliberation-punchline">
Without observable reality, meetings and politics become a substitute for knowledge.
</div>
</div>

<div class="deliberation-model">
<div class="deliberation-block">
<span>Bowtie</span>
<strong>Shared model</strong>
<p>Causes, risks, consequences, barriers, and assumptions become explicit.</p>
</div>
<div class="deliberation-plus">+</div>
<div class="deliberation-block">
<span>Habermas</span>
<strong>Structured argument</strong>
<p>Claims must be justified, located, challenged, and understood.</p>
</div>
<div class="deliberation-plus">+</div>
<div class="deliberation-block critical">
<span>System architecture</span>
<strong>Observable reality</strong>
<p>Metrics, events, tests, monitoring, and feedback make claims decidable.</p>
</div>
</div>


<div class="references">
Theory anchor: Jürgen Habermas, communicative action and deliberation. Applied reading: Alwin Mark, <a href="https://onlu.ch/deliberation-statt-endlosschleife/">Deliberation statt Endlosschleife</a>, ONLU AG.
</div>

<aside class="notes">
Connect Habermas to the enterprise wound: deliberation requires shared
understanding and justifiable claims. In practice, this only works if the system
produces observable reality. Otherwise meetings replace missing observability.
</aside>

---

## 06. Ivory Tower Architecture

<p class="slide-kicker">Architecture principles fail when they are not connected to implementation feedback.</p>

<div class="architecture-wound">
<div class="architecture-card">
<strong>Architects do not get feedback</strong>
Principles are defined far away from the friction of delivery, runtime behavior, incidents, and developer experience.
</div>

<div class="architecture-card">
<strong>Developers do not apply principles</strong>
Architecture becomes documentation, not a working constraint inside daily decisions, code reviews, tests, or releases.
</div>

<div class="architecture-card critical">
<strong>Trade-offs disappear</strong>
Principles sound like pure improvement. Coupling, cost, migration effort, latency, ownership, and operational risk stay hidden.
</div>
</div>

<div class="architecture-punchline">
Architecture without feedback becomes belief. Architecture without trade-offs becomes theater.
</div>

<aside class="notes">
Press the wound: architecture is often communicated as principles and target
states, but not tested as a living system. If developers do not feed back where
principles fail, and architects do not expose negative consequences, architecture
turns into documentation instead of decision capability.
</aside>

---

## 07. Responsibility Without Control

<p class="slide-kicker">Teams are accountable for outcomes they cannot fully influence.</p>

<div class="responsibility-bpm">
<div class="responsibility-lane team">
<div class="responsibility-lane-label">Team responsibility</div>
<div class="responsibility-flow">
<div class="responsibility-token">Own outcome</div>
<div class="responsibility-arrow">-></div>
<div class="responsibility-token">Find constraint</div>
<div class="responsibility-arrow">-></div>
<div class="responsibility-token blocked">Cannot change it</div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="responsibility-lane control">
<div class="responsibility-lane-label">Central control</div>
<div class="responsibility-flow">
<div class="responsibility-token">Decision rights</div>
<div class="responsibility-token">Architecture boundary</div>
<div class="responsibility-token">Budget / risk gate</div>
</div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="responsibility-effects">
<span>Escalation becomes the operating model.</span>
<span>Teams optimize locally to survive.</span>
<span>Management becomes the delivery bottleneck.</span>
</div>

<aside class="notes">
This is a Noesis problem: responsibility is delegated faster than authority,
context, and system-level influence. Teams are blamed for outcomes they cannot
shape. This connects hierarchy, architecture, autonomy, and decision boundaries.
</aside>

---

## 08. Survival of the Fastest

<p class="slide-kicker">The real problem is not one bad decision. It is organizational decision latency.</p>

<div class="survival-slide">
<div class="survival-core">
<strong>Organizations become too slow for their own reality.</strong>
<span>Problems are known, strategy is written, architecture is documented, meetings happen, and responsibility is assigned. But feedback does not move fast enough to change decisions.</span>
</div>

<div class="survival-anchors">
<div>Write-only strategy</div>
<div>Different communication codes</div>
<div>Meeting loops</div>
<div>Ivory tower architecture</div>
<div>Responsibility without control</div>
<div>Decisions without feedback</div>
</div>

<div class="survival-punchline">
Slow organizations do not fail because they lack ideas. They fail because reality reaches decisions too late.
</div>
</div>
